text: Our approach to the operation was using three robotic ports plus an assistant port with the fenestrated bipolar forceps through port one.
the camera in port 2 and the permanent quartery spatula through port 3.
Initially we positioned the robot in a pelvic position with the instruments facing downwards to give us a really good picture of the diaphragm.
We started by inspecting the diaphragm and removing all deposits of endometrial tissue that we could see.
Here with one nodule here very easily removed.
And this position really giving us a very nice overview of the diaphragm itself.
And you can see these marked endometrial deposits and this large area of fenestration through with the liver visible underneath.
And this area of nodularity that was seen in this scan just at the top of the picture here.
We started by using the bipolar cautery and the spatula to resect the abnormal tissue present on the diaphragm.
here retracting with the bipolar forceps and then using the spatula to dissect with the assistant holding the suction catheter here in the left hand side of the picture.
And again the clarity that we get from the robot for seeing in slightly awkward places really does make these operations far more straightforward.
Here we are completing the resection of this part of the diaphragm.
You can see how nice the spatula is to be able to dissect gently away from the adhesions towards the liver as well.
And then staying in this position, we then started doing a pleurectomy in the costophrenic recess.
So again, part of the pleura, which is slightly awkward to get to using a normal VATS approach, but by using this pelvic position, we undertook a really good pleurectomy here.
Before we then moved on to repair the diaphragm.
Now we repaired the diaphragm using a layered
Ethibond stitch.
Initially, we're running continuous stitch and then reinforcing with multiple interrupted stitches.
As you can see, stitching with a robot is just so similar to stitching normally using instruments.
Far, far more straightforward than doing this procedure VATS.
You can see the other areas of the diaphragm where we have resected small endometrial deposits.
And here is the final picture after the running stitch, again reinforced with several further interrupted stitches.
Before we then introduce the Teflon patch to complete the diaphragm repair.
and the patch being somewhat loosely stitched in place on top of the diaphragm with multiple interrupted stitches.
And we achieved a nice final appearance, as we can see here.
Now at this point in the operation,
We then undocked the robot and then redocked through the same ports in the normal thoracic approach.
So there we in that initial picture looking up towards the apex and now starting our pleurectomy and the anterior chest wall.
And the robot again, just allowing a degree of accuracy and visualization here, which is unmatched with other approaches.

we undertook an extensive pleurectomy in all areas of the chest wall and you can see here the almost final approach a small patch left here on the front which we then removed prior to inserting a single chest drain and inflating the lung prior to closure so that's the end of our video for today i hope you found it interesting and thank you very much
